query: mechanism of ganglionic transmission in autonomic ganglia

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Autonomic Nervous System -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Norepinephrine gets released by postganglionic neurons of the sympathetic nervous system, which binds to and activates adrenergic receptors. These receptors further divide into alpha11 (G coupled receptor), alpha-2 (G  coupled receptor), beta-1 (G coupled receptor), and beta-2 and beta-3 (G and G coupled receptor).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Autonomic Nervous System -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The ANS exerts its control through chemical messengers known as neurotransmitters. The neurotransmitters involved in the ANS are acetylcholine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. Preganglionic neurons of the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions and postganglionic neurons of the parasympathetic nervous system utilize acetylcholine (ACh). Postganglionic neurons of the sympathetic nervous system use norepinephrine and epinephrine. Although, there are exceptions to this as described below.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Autonomic Nervous System -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Epinephrine is released into systemic circulation by chromaffin cells within the adrenal gland, specifically the adrenal medulla. The sympathetic nervous system splanchnic nerves synapse on chromaffin cells and stimulate them to release epinephrine using the signal messenger acetylcholine. This feature of the adrenal gland is an important component of the sympathetic nervous system as it allows systemic effects to occur on major organ systems. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Nervous Control -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The second aspect included in the neural control of the GI tract is the autonomic system. This system is comprised of the sympathetic and parasympathetic systems. In the case of the GI tract, the parasympathetic tract is typically excitatory. The parasympathetic system exerts its effects primarily via the vagus (innervates the esophagus, stomach, pancreas, upper large intestine) and pelvic nerves (innervates the lower large intestine, rectum, and anus.) The vagus nerve regulates tone and volume by activating the enteric motor neurons. They do this by synapsing on the myenteric motor neurons and exhibiting inhibitory action via nitric oxide or excitatory action via acetylcholine and neurokinins. The enteric motor neurons, including the myenteric plexus, then synapse on the ICCs within muscle bundles. These cells then communicate via gap junctions to the smooth muscle cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Nicotinic Ganglionic Blocker (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine (ACh) is a neurotransmitter released from cholinergic neurons which binds two types of receptors: muscarinic and nicotinic ACh receptors (mAChRs and nAChRs, respectively). [5] Nicotinic ganglion receptors found in the autonomic ganglia are involved in both sympathetic and parasympathetic transmission. These receptors classify as neuronal nicotinic (Nn) cholinergic receptors, and they are on postganglionic efferent neurons. [1] Nn receptors are Cys-loop ligand-gated ion channels that, upon binding ACh, undergo a conformational change and become permeable to ions such as Na+, K+, and Ca2+, resulting in signal propagation through the postganglionic efferent neuron. [5] Nicotinic ganglion blockers exhibit a noncompetitive antagonism of Nn receptors, which results in blockade of signal transduction. [2] The blockade of Nn receptors at the level of the autonomic ganglia results in decreased sympathetic output, which reduces blood pressure, thereby producing the therapeutic effect. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Autonomic Nervous System -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: ACh is released by all preganglionic neurons, regardless of their involvement in the parasympathetic or sympathetic nervous system. ACh binds to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, a ligand-gated ion channel, on postganglionic neurons. Once ACh binds, the ion channel is open to allow the flow of ions, specifically sodium and calcium, which induces an excitatory postsynaptic potential, and results in the propagation of a neuronal signal down the axon. [5] ACh also gets released by postganglionic neurons of the parasympathetic nervous system. Here, ACh binds to muscarinic acetylcholine receptors on the target site, which are G-protein-coupled receptors. These act to perform the functions of the parasympathetic nervous system listed above. It is important to note that ACh also gets released by postganglionic neurons of sweat glands, and binds to and activates muscarinic receptors. This activity is an exception to the sympathetic nervous system, which normally involves norepinephrine as its chemical messenger released from postganglionic neurons. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Synapse -- Mechanism -- Synapses
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine (ACh): One of the most important neurotransmitters found in multiple synapses in the body, including, but not limited to, the neuromuscular junction, autonomic ganglia, caudate nucleus, and the limbic system. Generally, ACh is an excitatory neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction and in the autonomic ganglia. In the brain, Ach is synthesized in the basal nucleus of Meynert.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastrointestinal Nervous Control -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Sympathetic activity in the GI tract is fundamentally inhibitory. These fibers originate from spinal cord levels T-8 through L-2. These fibers then synapse on the pre-vertebral ganglia and continue onward to finally synapse on the myenteric and submucosal plexuses, which respond to manipulate smooth muscle cells, secretory cells, and endocrine cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Synapse -- Mechanism -- Synapses
retrieved_docs.content: Norepinephrine (NE): The most important molecule in the sympathetic nervous system signaling, except for the sweat glands. NE is mainly found in the locus coeruleus and lateral tegmental nuclei in the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Peristalsis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The parasympathetic nervous system stimulates peristalsis via the myenteric plexus. The myenteric plexus's afferent (sensory) nerves deliver information to interneurons within the plexus. Interneurons communicate with efferent nerves, stimulating an action potential (spike-wave) within smooth muscle cells. The afferent nerves are stimulated by 2 mechanisms: reflexive (stretch or chemoreceptors) or parasympathetic stimulation (via acetylcholine).

retrieved_docs.title: Autonomic Dysfunction -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The autonomic nervous system (ANS) is a subcomponent of the peripheral nervous system (PNS) that regulates involuntary physiologic processes, including blood pressure, heart rate, respiration, digestion, and sexual arousal. It comprises sympathetic, parasympathetic, and enteric nervous systems, which are three anatomically distinct divisions. The sympathetic nervous system (SNS), as well as the parasympathetic nervous system (PNS), contain afferent fibers that provide sensory input and efferent fibers that provide motor output to the central nervous system (CNS). The SNS and PNS motor pathways incorporate a two-neuron series: a pre-ganglionic neuron with a cell body in the CNS and a post-ganglionic neuron with a cell body in the periphery that innervates target tissues [1] . The enteric nervous system (ENS) is a large, web-like structure capable of functioning independently of the remainder of the nervous system. [2] [3] It is chiefly responsible for regulating digestive processes and contains over a hundred million neurons of over fifteen morphologies, greater than the sum of all other peripheral ganglia. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholinergic Receptors -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The generation of calcium and protein kinase C is responsible for further activation of downstream events, which produce the overall effect of receptor activation. Alternatively, the M2 receptor is an inhibitory g-protein (Gi) GPCR, which upon ligand binding, leads to the inhibition of adenylate cyclase, resulting in a decrease in the second messenger molecule cAMP. [13] . The downregulation of cAMP levels in the cell opposes the activation of a separate stimulatory GPCR known as Gs. cAMP production occurs within the cell through activation of the stimulatory g-protein (G) GPCR, which constitutes the mechanism of beta-adrenergic receptor stimulation of the cell, a component of the sympathetic nervous system. [13] As a result of separate receptor physiology, cholinergic receptors produce vastly different chemical messaging systems in the cells of their effector organs due to differences in signal transduction. Knowing these differences helps one understand the roles each receptor plays within its specific tissue type. At the neuromuscular junction, rapid signal transduction is necessary, while within the neuronal tissue, activation of the M1 receptor may lead to a longer, more sustained response through activation of gene transcription.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adenosine Triphosphate -- Function -- Purinergic Signaling
retrieved_docs.content: Purinergic signaling is a form of extracellular paracrine signaling that is mediated by purine nucleotides, including ATP. This process commonly entails the activation of purinergic receptors on cells within proximity, thereby transducing signals to regulate intracellular processes. ATP is released from vesicular stores and is regulated by IP3 and other common exocytotic regulatory mechanisms. ATP is co-stored and co-released among neurotransmitters, further supporting the notion that ATP is a necessary mediator of purinergic neurotransmission in both sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves. ATP can induce several purinergic responses, including control of autonomic functions, neural glia interactions, pain, and control of vessel tone. [9] [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Lumbar Sympathetic Chain -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic structure: Autonomic ganglia contain the ganglionic neurons, satellite glial cells, and small intensely fluorescent cells. Ganglionic neurons form many axodendritic synapses with preganglionic axons. Satellite glial cells are closely associated with neurons; they may influence synaptic transmission. Small intensely fluorescent cells possess several granules that contain noradrenaline, dopamine, and serotonin. Sympathetic ganglia contain multipolar neurons, preganglionic sympathetic axons synapse with many postganglionic neurons which is responsible for the widespread effects of the sympathetic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Cholinergic Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is a major neurotransmitter in the body. Depending on the type of receptors through which it undergoes mediation, the peripheral actions of acetylcholine classify as working on muscarinic (M1, M2, M3, M4, M5) or nicotinic (Nm, Nn) receptors. M1 receptors are present on the gastric parietal cells and in the central nervous system. M2 receptors are present on the heart, visceral smooth muscle. M3 receptors on the smooth muscle, exocrine glands, and receptors of the bladder. Nicotinic receptors are present in the central nervous system, adrenal medulla, autonomic ganglia, and neuromuscular junction. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Autonomic Pharmacology -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Muscarinic-4 (M4) and muscarinic-5 (M5): This is primarily located in the central nervous system (CNS), specifically the forebrain and substantia nigra, respectively. Nicotinic-N (NN): This is located in the postsynaptic dendrites of both sympathetic and parasympathetic postganglionic neurons. Effects mediated by Na + /K + depolarization include increased neurotransmission. Nicotinic-M (NM): This is found in neuromuscular endplates of skeletal muscles. Effects mediated by Na + /K + depolarization include skeletal muscle contraction. For dopamine receptors, which are involved in both SNS and PSNS processes: [30] Dopamine 1 to 5 (D1-5): This is primarily located in the CNS, except for dopamine-1 receptors, which are also found in renal vasculature. Effects mediated by the cAMP pathway include renal artery vasodilation, increased renal blood flow, and modulation of neuroendocrine signaling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and bethanechol are both classified as parasympathomimetics, a group of agents that mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. By stimulating cholinergic pathways, these drugs enhance parasympathetic tone, which can be therapeutically useful in conditions where increased muscarinic activity is desired. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action despite being used for different clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 824
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic antagonists are classified as a type of neurotransmitter antagonist, reflecting their role in blocking the actions of catecholamine neurotransmitters at adrenergic receptors. Within this pharmacologic group are several specific agents, including dihydroergocristine, its mesylate salt (dihydroergocristine mesylate), fentolamine, and oxidopamine. These compounds share the common mechanism of inhibiting adrenergic signaling, which can be utilized therapeutically to modulate sympathetic nervous system activity in various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin and edrofonium are both classified as parasympathomimetic agents, meaning they mimic the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system. As members of this pharmacologic class, they enhance cholinergic transmission and can be used to support functions mediated by acetylcholine, such as cognition, memory, and certain autonomic processes. Recognizing both galantamin and edrofonium as parasympathomimetics helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action and potential therapeutic applications in conditions where augmentation of parasympathetic activity is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is recognized as having a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, indicating that it functions as a neurotransmitter within the nervous system. This classification underscores acetylcholine’s role in transmitting signals between nerve cells and across neuromuscular junctions, which is fundamental to processes such as muscle contraction, autonomic nervous system regulation, and cognitive functions. Understanding acetylcholine’s position as a neurotransmitter is essential for comprehending its impact on both normal physiological signaling and various clinical conditions where its pathways may be altered.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: inhibiting transmission at autonomic ganglia. The fixed‑dose combination of chlordiazepoxide and clidinium functions as a cholinergic antagonist, interfering with acetylcholine‑mediated signaling. Parasympatholytic agents are linked to anti‑emetic therapy because inhibition of parasympathetic activity helps prevent nausea and vomiting. The combination product chlordiazepoxide / clidinium is related to chlordiazepoxide hydrochloride, reflecting its inclusion of the hydrochloride salt. Collectively, these entities illustrate how individual drugs—such as aclidinium bromide for COPD, dihydroergotamine for adrenergic antagonism, the diverse anticholinergic and antitussive agents, and the wide array of compounds used for functional gastrointestinal disorders—fit into larger pharmacological families. Shared mechanisms (primarily receptor antagonism), hierarchical classifications, therapeutic roles, and safety considerations inform patient management across respiratory, gastrointestinal,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1450
retrieved_docs.content: Methacholin is identified as a specific bronchoconstrictive agent that belongs to the broader class of autonomic substances. Within the autonomic system, parasympathomimetics serve as a parent category, indicating that methacholin’s pharmacologic effects are mediated through parasympathetic stimulation. Because methacholin induces bronchoconstriction, the mechanism of action of adrenergic beta‑antagonists is contraindicated when methacholin is used, as beta‑blockers would oppose the desired therapeutic effect. The hierarchical relationships show that autonomic agents encompass both parasympathomimetic and bronchoconstrictive agents, situating methacholin at the intersection of these categories and highlighting the need to avoid beta‑adrenergic antagonists in patients receiving methacholin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 99
retrieved_docs.content: and lobelin is categorized as a ganglion‑blocking agent (gangliové blokátory). The hierarchical structure thus connects anticholinergic agents, adrenergic antagonists, antitussives, opioid analgesics, peptide‑hormone receptor antagonists, and related subclasses, clarifying mechanisms of action (acetylcholine or catecholamine blockade, peptide‑hormone receptor inhibition), therapeutic uses (bronchodilation, parasympathetic inhibition, cough suppression, cardiovascular modulation, analgesia), and safety considerations essential for clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Dopamine is classified as a sympathomimetic, meaning it mimics the actions of the sympathetic nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1581
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympatomimetic agent, placing it within the group of drugs that imitate the actions of the parasympathetic nervous system. Parasympatomimetika themselves are a subset of autonomní látky (autonomic substances), which encompass all pharmacologic agents that affect autonomic nervous system function. By acting as a parasympatomimetic, galantamin contributes to the modulation of cholinergic pathways and therefore shares the broader physiological effects characteristic of autonomic substances. Understanding galantamin’s position within the parasympatomimetic class and its relationship to autonomní látky aids clinicians in anticipating its pharmacodynamic profile and potential therapeutic or adverse effects related to autonomic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1110
retrieved_docs.content: Gangliové blokátory are a class of pharmacological agents that block transmission at autonomic ganglia, thereby modulating sympathetic and parasympathetic activity. Lobellin is a specific drug that belongs to this class, representing an example of a gangliové blokátory. This relationship indicates that lobellin inherits the characteristic ganglion‑blocking properties of gangliové blokátory and is used in clinical contexts where inhibition of autonomic ganglionic transmission is desired.

Collectively, these classifications, mechanisms of action and interaction profiles delineate the therapeutic and research relevance of the listed agents in managing glaucoma, cardiovascular disease, lipid disorders, varicose veins, hematologic conditions and autonomic function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2172
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclopentamine and Nafazolin are both classified as plain sympathomimetics, meaning each drug is a member of the sympathomimetic drug class without additional modifiers. As members of the “Sympathomimetics, plain” group, cyclopentamine and nafazolin share the core pharmacologic characteristic of stimulating adrenergic receptors, which underlies their ability to mimic the effects of the sympathetic nervous system. This shared classification informs clinicians about their expected mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles, facilitating appropriate selection and monitoring when these agents are used in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine and acetylcholine are both classified as neurotransmitters, meaning they serve as chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses in the nervous system. As neurotransmitters, each plays distinct but complementary roles in modulating neuronal activity, influencing processes such as sleep regulation, cardiovascular function, muscle contraction, and cognitive function. Recognizing that adenosine and acetylcholine share this broader classification helps clinicians understand their contributions to normal physiology and to various disorders, guiding therapeutic strategies that target neurotransmitter pathways for conditions like arrhythmias, neurodegenerative diseases, and sleep disturbances.
